notes.note: Lucius Calpurnius Bibulus was the son of Marcus Calpurnius Bibulus (consul 59 BC) and Porcia, the sister of Cato the Younger; Porcia was also Brutus's half-sister (Cato was Brutus's maternal uncle), making Bibulus Brutus's cousin by marriage.

When a consul died in office, the surviving consul held elections (comitia) to fill the vacancy for the remainder of the year; Bibulus wished to be elected suffect consul to replace Pansa, who died of wounds sustained at Forum Gallorum.

notes.anchor: That nomination we ask of you
notes.note: Roman electoral procedure required that a magistrate formally announce (nominare) a candidate's name in the comitia; Cicero, as the most senior statesman in Rome, had the political standing to nominate Bibulus effectively.

Gnaeus Domitius Ahenobarbus (later consul 32 BC) and Marcus Appuleius are here commended as additional candidates for office; both were young men associated with Brutus's cause in the East who stood for elections in Rome in 43 BC.

notes.anchor: he can grow up to answer to the praises of you few
notes.note: Brutus closes with an appeal to Cicero's pride: Bibulus can fulfil the legacy of the small circle of great men — Cicero, Cato, Brutus himself — who have upheld Republican ideals against both Caesar and Antony.
